sources.full_name: Financial Stability Board standards and policy documents
sources.coverage_note: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes, TLAC, G-SIB assessment, climate risk, crypto-asset regulation, shadow banking, OTC derivatives reform.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 1 - Scope
provisions.text: Any financial institution that could be systemically significant or critical if it fails should be subject to a resolution regime that has the attributes set out in this document. The regime should be clear and transparent as to the circumstances in which and conditions on which resolution powers may be exercised, including the point of non-viability.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 2 - Resolution Authority
provisions.text: Each jurisdiction should have a designated administrative authority (resolution authority) responsible for exercising the resolution powers over firms within the scope of the resolution regime. The resolution authority should have operational independence, transparent processes, sound governance, and adequate resources.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 3 - Resolution Powers
provisions.text: Resolution authorities should have at their disposal a broad range of resolution powers, including: transfer or sale of assets and liabilities to a third party; establishment of a temporary bridge institution; separation of non-performing assets into a separate asset management vehicle; bail-in through write-down or conversion of liabilities; and temporary stay on exercise of early termination rights.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 4 - Set-off, Netting, Collateralisation, Segregation
provisions.text: Resolution regimes should provide for: temporary stays on early termination rights in financial contracts; recognition of set-off and netting arrangements; continued performance of contracts essential to the operation of the firm in resolution; and protection of client assets through segregation requirements.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 5 - Safeguards
provisions.text: Resolution regimes should include safeguards to ensure that resolution powers are exercised in a manner that respects the hierarchy of claims and the rights of shareholders and creditors. The 'no creditor worse off' (NCWO) safeguard requires that no shareholder or creditor should be worse off in resolution than in liquidation. There should be a mechanism for compensation where the NCWO safeguard is breached.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 6 - Funding of Firms in Resolution
provisions.text: Jurisdictions should have in place privately-financed deposit insurance or resolution funds, or a funding mechanism with ex post recovery from the industry, to provide temporary funding to facilitate the resolution of firms. The use of public funds should be a last resort, and any public funding should be recoverable from the industry.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 7 - Legal Framework for Cross-Border Cooperation
provisions.text: The statutory framework for resolution should provide for coordination and cooperation with relevant foreign resolution authorities. Jurisdictions should provide for transparent and expedited processes to give effect to foreign resolution measures, either by way of a legal framework for recognition of foreign resolution actions or by taking measures under the domestic resolution regime that support and are consistent with the resolution measures taken by the foreign resolution authority.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 8 - Crisis Management Groups (CMGs)
provisions.text: Home and host authorities of all G-SIFIs should maintain Crisis Management Groups (CMGs) with the objective of enhancing preparedness for, and facilitating the management and resolution of, a cross-border financial crisis affecting the firm. CMGs should include the supervisory authorities, central banks, resolution authorities, finance ministries, and deposit insurers of jurisdictions that are material to the resolution of the firm.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 9 - Institution-Specific Cross-Border Cooperation Agreements
provisions.text: For each G-SIFI, institution-specific cross-border cooperation agreements should be in place between the relevant resolution authorities, covering at a minimum: the roles and responsibilities of the authorities involved; procedures for information sharing; the process for activating and coordinating the implementation of resolution actions; and the terms of funding in resolution.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 10 - Resolvability Assessments
provisions.text: Jurisdictions should require that all G-SIFIs and any other financial institution that could be systemically significant undergo regular resolvability assessments. Resolvability assessments should evaluate whether feasible and credible resolution strategies and plans exist, and whether it is feasible to resolve the firm in a way that protects systemically important functions without severe systemic disruption and without exposing taxpayers to loss.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 11 - Recovery and Resolution Planning
provisions.text: All G-SIFIs should be required to prepare and maintain recovery plans (living wills) and resolution plans. Recovery plans should set out options to restore financial strength and viability when the firm comes under severe stress. Resolution plans should set out how a firm could be resolved in a manner consistent with the resolution objectives. Plans should be updated at least annually and following significant changes to the firm's structure or business.

provisions.part: Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes
provisions.title: KA 12 - Access to Information and Information Sharing
provisions.text: Jurisdictions should ensure that no legal, regulatory or policy impediments exist that hinder the appropriate exchange of information, including firm-specific information, between supervisory authorities, central banks, resolution authorities, finance ministries and the public authorities responsible for deposit insurance. The exchange of information should be able to take place in normal times and in times of crisis, and should cover both domestic and cross-border information sharing.

provisions.title: TLAC Minimum Requirements
provisions.text: Global systemically important banks (G-SIBs) must maintain a minimum TLAC requirement. The minimum TLAC must be at least 18% of the resolution group's risk-weighted assets (RWA) and at least 6.75% of the resolution group's Basel III leverage ratio exposure measure. TLAC must be met with a combination of regulatory capital and eligible external long-term debt that can be written down or converted to equity in resolution.

provisions.title: TLAC Eligibility Criteria
provisions.text: To qualify as TLAC, instruments must: be unsecured; have a remaining maturity of at least one year (or be perpetual); not be subject to set-off or netting rights that would undermine their loss-absorbing capacity; not be funded directly or indirectly by the resolution entity or a related party; not be subject to any arrangement that legally or economically enhances the seniority of the claim in insolvency; and be issued and maintained in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction whose resolution regime satisfies the Key Attributes.

provisions.title: Internal TLAC
provisions.text: Material sub-groups of G-SIBs that are not themselves resolution entities must maintain internal TLAC. Internal TLAC must be between 75% and 90% of the external TLAC requirement that would apply if the material sub-group were a resolution group. Internal TLAC should be pre-positioned within the group structure to ensure that losses in a subsidiary can be passed up to the resolution entity without placing the subsidiary into local insolvency proceedings.

provisions.title: G-SIB Identification Methodology
provisions.text: The G-SIB assessment methodology uses an indicator-based approach with five equally weighted categories (20% each): size (total exposures as defined by the Basel III leverage ratio), interconnectedness (intra-financial system assets, liabilities, securities outstanding), substitutability/financial institution infrastructure (assets under custody, payments activity, underwritten transactions), complexity (notional amount of OTC derivatives, Level 3 assets, trading and available-for-sale securities), and cross-jurisdictional activity (cross-jurisdictional claims and liabilities).

provisions.title: G-SIB Bucket Allocation
provisions.text: G-SIBs are allocated to buckets that determine the additional loss absorbency (capital surcharge) requirements. Bucket 1: 1.0% additional CET1; Bucket 2: 1.5%; Bucket 3: 2.0%; Bucket 4: 2.5%; Bucket 5 (empty): 3.5%. The bucket allocation is based on the aggregate score from the indicator-based methodology. The list of G-SIBs and their bucket allocations is published annually by the FSB in November.

provisions.title: FSB Roadmap for Addressing Climate-Related Financial Risks
provisions.text: The FSB roadmap for addressing climate-related financial risks focuses on four areas: firm-level disclosures (building on TCFD recommendations, now incorporated into ISSB standards); data availability (improving availability of consistent, comparable climate data); vulnerability analysis (developing climate scenario analysis and stress testing approaches); and regulatory and supervisory approaches (coordinating approaches across jurisdictions). The roadmap promotes the use of climate scenario analysis across the financial system.

provisions.title: Supervisory and Regulatory Approaches to Climate Risk
provisions.text: The FSB has published a framework for supervisory and regulatory approaches to climate-related risks in the financial sector. The framework covers: integration of climate risks into existing risk management frameworks; use of scenario analysis and stress testing for climate risks; development of climate-related disclosure requirements; consideration of climate risks in microprudential and macroprudential supervision; and cross-border coordination on climate risk oversight.

provisions.title: Climate Scenario Analysis
provisions.text: Financial institutions and supervisors should use climate scenario analysis to assess the impact of physical and transition risks on financial stability. Scenarios should cover at least an orderly transition scenario (net zero by 2050), a disorderly transition scenario (delayed and abrupt action), and a hot house world scenario (no additional policy action). Scenario analysis should cover both short-term and long-term time horizons and should include second-round effects and feedback loops.

provisions.title: High-Level Recommendations for Crypto-Asset Activities
provisions.text: The FSB's high-level recommendations for the regulation, supervision and oversight of crypto-asset activities and markets are based on the principle of 'same activity, same risk, same regulation'. The 9 recommendations cover: regulatory powers and tools; comprehensive regulatory framework; cross-border cooperation; governance framework requirements; risk management for crypto-asset service providers; data collection and reporting; disclosure requirements; addressing risks arising from interconnections with the traditional financial system; and multi-function crypto-asset intermediaries.

provisions.title: High-Level Recommendations for Global Stablecoin Arrangements
provisions.text: The FSB's recommendations for global stablecoin (GSC) arrangements cover: authorities should have appropriate powers to regulate GSC arrangements; GSC arrangements should have comprehensive governance frameworks; GSC arrangements should have effective risk management; GSC arrangements should maintain reserve assets and redemption rights; GSC arrangements should meet data storage, security, and privacy requirements; GSC arrangements should provide for recovery and resolution planning; and GSC arrangements should comply with applicable AML/CFT requirements.

provisions.title: Central Clearing of Standardised OTC Derivatives
provisions.text: All standardised OTC derivative contracts should be traded on exchanges or electronic trading platforms and cleared through central counterparties (CCPs) by end-2012. Non-centrally cleared contracts should be subject to higher capital requirements and mandatory margin requirements. Trade reporting of all OTC derivatives to trade repositories should be mandatory.

provisions.title: Margin Requirements for Non-Centrally Cleared Derivatives
provisions.text: Non-centrally cleared derivatives should be subject to mandatory exchange of initial margin (IM) and variation margin (VM). Initial margin should be collected by both parties on a gross basis and should not be netted. Collected initial margin should be held in segregated accounts. Variation margin should be exchanged bilaterally. The framework includes thresholds for the application of IM requirements based on notional amounts.

provisions.title: Trade Reporting to Trade Repositories
provisions.text: All OTC derivative transactions should be reported to trade repositories (TRs). Trade repositories should be subject to regulation and oversight. Data collected by trade repositories should be accessible to relevant authorities for the purposes of monitoring systemic risk, market abuse, and ensuring financial stability. The FSB has developed guidance on the governance, access, and use of TR data.

provisions.title: Shadow Banking Monitoring and Policy Framework
provisions.text: The FSB monitors the global shadow banking system through its annual Global Monitoring Report on Non-Bank Financial Intermediation (NBFI). The monitoring framework covers: mapping of the NBFI sector by economic function; assessment of systemic risks; and policy recommendations for specific shadow banking activities. Five workstreams address: banks' interactions with shadow banking entities; money market funds; other shadow banking entities; securitisation; and securities lending and repos.

provisions.title: Securities Financing Transactions (SFTs)
provisions.text: The FSB has developed a regulatory framework for haircuts on non-centrally cleared securities financing transactions (SFTs). The framework includes: numerical haircut floors for non-bank-to-non-bank SFTs; qualitative standards for methodologies used by market participants to calculate haircuts; and a framework for central clearing of SFTs where appropriate. The framework aims to reduce the procyclicality of SFT markets and limit the build-up of excessive leverage.

provisions.title: Enhancing the Resilience of Non-Bank Financial Intermediation
provisions.text: Following the March 2020 market turmoil, the FSB developed a programme to enhance the resilience of NBFI. The programme covers: money market fund reform (addressing structural vulnerabilities); open-ended fund liquidity management (alignment of fund terms with asset liquidity); margining practices (reducing procyclicality in margin calls); bond market liquidity (enhancing resilience of core bond markets); and cross-cutting issues (leverage, interconnectedness, data gaps). The FSB coordinates national implementation of NBFI reforms.

provisions.title: Cyber Incident Reporting Framework
provisions.text: The FSB has developed recommendations for achieving greater convergence in cyber incident reporting. The framework includes: a common terminology for cyber incidents; common incident reporting requirements covering initial notification, interim updates, and final reports; a common format for incident reporting (FIRE - Format for Incident Reporting Exchange); and guidance on thresholds and timelines for reporting. The framework aims to reduce the reporting burden on financial institutions while improving the quality of information available to authorities.

provisions.title: Third-Party Risk Management and Oversight
provisions.text: The FSB has published a toolkit for financial authorities and financial institutions on third-party risk management and outsourcing. The toolkit covers: governance arrangements for third-party dependencies; risk assessment and due diligence processes; contractual provisions (service levels, audit rights, business continuity, data protection); ongoing monitoring of third-party service providers; exit strategies and substitutability; and specific considerations for cloud services and critical third parties.

provisions.title: Principles for Sound Compensation Practices
provisions.text: The FSB Principles for Sound Compensation Practices and their Implementation Standards require that: compensation practices support sound risk management and do not encourage excessive risk-taking; compensation structures are aligned with the firm's risk and time horizon; the board of directors should actively oversee the design and operation of the compensation system; compensation outcomes should be symmetric with risk outcomes (malus and clawback); a substantial portion of variable compensation should be deferred and subject to risk adjustment.

provisions.title: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Financial Services
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed the financial stability implications of the use of AI and machine learning in financial services. Key areas of focus include: risks from lack of explainability/interpretability of AI models; risks from data dependencies (quality, bias, representativeness); risks from concentration and third-party dependencies; risks from cyber attacks on AI systems; and governance challenges around AI-driven decision-making. Supervisors should ensure that existing regulatory frameworks are applied effectively to AI-driven activities.

provisions.title: The Financial Stability Risks of Decentralised Finance
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed the financial stability risks posed by decentralised finance (DeFi). Key risks include: operational vulnerabilities (smart contract bugs, oracle manipulation, governance attacks); liquidity and maturity mismatches in DeFi protocols; leverage and interconnectedness risks; concentration risks in DeFi infrastructure; and gaps in the regulatory perimeter. The FSB recommends that jurisdictions apply comprehensive and consistent regulatory frameworks to DeFi activities, using a functional approach that focuses on the economic substance of activities rather than technology.

definitions.definition: The total amount of equity and eligible debt that can be used to absorb losses and recapitalize a G-SIB in the event of its failure, ensuring that critical functions can be continued without recourse to public funds.

A financial institution whose distress or disorderly failure, because of its size, complexity, interconnectedness, lack of substitutability, or global scope, would cause significant disruption to the wider financial system and economic activity.

A resolution safeguard principle stating that no shareholder or creditor should receive less in resolution than they would have received had the firm been wound up under normal insolvency proceedings.
A group of home and host authorities of a G-SIFI established to enhance preparedness for, and facilitate the management and resolution of, a cross-border financial crisis affecting the firm.
